region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely sold across Singapore at hawker dessert stalls and kopitiams
food_subgroup: Silken soya bean curd (tofu fa) with ginger-rock sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has silken tofu (FOODON_03309998) but does not have a specific entry for tofu fa dessert served with ginger-rock sugar syrup. Silken tofu is used as the closest parent concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Silken soya bean curd pudding (tofu fa) with ginger-rock sugar syrup
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00YD (soybean curd / tofu) is the closest available parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the sweetened dessert preparation with ginger syrup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tofu fa dessert with ginger syrup. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific prepared food item.

preparation_method: coagulation with calcium sulphate (gypsum) or glucono-delta-lactone (GDL)
served with warm ginger-rock sugar syrup

serving.description: 1 bowl (300g) — approximately 250g tofu fa plus 50g ginger-rock sugar syrup
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving is one bowl of approximately 300g, comprising roughly 250g of silken soy curd scooped into a bowl and topped with 50g of warm ginger-rock sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu Fa / Beancurd Pudding (with ginger syrup) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tofu Fa / Beancurd Pudding with ginger syrup. This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Values reflect the composite dish including ginger syrup.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soy Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; soy milk and silken tofu GI values (~25–35)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for tofu fa with ginger syrup as a composite dish. GI of 30 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy milk and silken tofu (GI range ~25–35). The soy protein matrix dominates the glycemic response; the ginger-rock sugar syrup contributes additional carbohydrate but the protein and fat content of the soy curd moderates the overall glycemic impact. GL per 300g serving = (30 × 10.0 × 300/100) / 100 = 9.0g. This is consistent with the low-GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (22 mg/100g); calcium from gypsum coagulant supports vascular tone
hypertension.guidance: Tofu fa is one of the lowest-sodium desserts available at Singapore hawker stalls, delivering only 66 mg sodium per 300g bowl, making it highly suitable for individuals managing hypertension; the calcium content (68 mg/100g) from gypsum coagulation may additionally support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~30) and low GL per serving (9.0); soy isoflavones may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of only 9.0 per standard 300g bowl, tofu fa is a suitable dessert choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) have been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in clinical studies, and requesting less syrup further reduces the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), soy protein with cardioprotective isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tofu fa contains zero cholesterol and only 0.2g saturated fat per 100g, while providing soy protein and isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) that have demonstrated LDL-lowering and anti-atherogenic effects in peer-reviewed literature, making it a cardioprotective dessert option when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones and ginger gingerols/shogaols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy isoflavones in tofu fa have documented anti-inflammatory effects that may reduce proinflammatory cytokine production, and the ginger in the syrup contributes gingerols and shogaols — potent anti-inflammatory phytochemicals — making this dessert particularly beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tofu Fa (豆腐花, also known as Tau Fu Fa, Tau Huay, or Dau Fu Fa) is one of Singapore's most beloved and enduring traditional Chinese desserts, originating from Guangdong (Canton), China. It is a silky, wobbly, barely-set soy milk custard made by heating fresh soy milk to near-boiling and then coagulating it with calcium sulphate (gypsum) or glucono-delta-lactone (GDL). The result is far softer and more delicate than firm tofu — it trembles when touched and dissolves almost instantly on the tongue. The custard is scooped into bowls using a wide, flat ladle and served with a warm, fragrant ginger-rock sugar syrup that seeps into the curd, imparting a gentle sweetness and warming spice. It can also be served chilled. Tofu Fa is found at traditional Chinese dessert stalls, hawker centres, kopitiams, and in sealed cups at convenience stores across Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a standout dessert: high in calcium (68 mg/100g) from gypsum coagulation, a good source of soy protein (3.2g/100g), and rich in soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) with documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. The ginger syrup contributes anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols. With a low GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of only 9.0 per 300g bowl, it is one of the most clinically positive dessert options for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Sodium is extremely low at 22 mg/100g, cholesterol is zero, and saturated fat is negligible at 0.2g/100g. It is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: is tofu fa good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. The entry is an exact match for Tofu Fa / Beancurd Pudding with ginger syrup. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous soy food entries (silken tofu / soy milk, GI ~25–35), which is the approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology anchors are defensible with honest gap notes.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal products or fortification. Vitamin D is confirmed zero as tofu fa is not fortified and soy milk used in traditional hawker preparation is unfortified. Trans fat is confirmed zero per source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by HPB Foodo source; set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. The duplicate portion entry (250g) in the input has been consolidated; the primary serving is 300g (1 standard bowl). The FoodEx2 code A00YD is used as the closest parent for soybean curd/tofu; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for the sweetened dessert preparation. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database soy food entries and should be updated if a direct composite-dish GI measurement becomes available.
